corruptions.original_text: Temporal split with stratification by traffic source. Chronologically divide the 18-month dataset into three periods: (1) months 1–12 (training), (2) months 13–15 (validation), (3) months 16–18 (test). Within each period, stratify by top 10 acquisition channels (organic, paid_search, paid_social, referral, direct, etc.) to ensure each channel is represented proportionally in train/val/test. This temporal split prevents information leakage (a session's future events cannot inform its training) and respects the time-series nature of marketplace dynamics, campaign seasonality, and model deployment drift. Stratification ensures the baseline and sequential model are each evaluated fairly across channel segments, preventing spurious gains that exploit channel-specific imbalances.
corruptions.corrupted_text: Partition the 18-month session table into training (70%), validation (15%), and test (15%) using stratified sampling over acquisition channel crossed with calendar month. This keeps each split matched on both channel mix and seasonal demand regimes, which is important because conversion propensity varies materially across campaign periods and holiday peaks. Matching the month-channel composition across splits reduces variance in model comparison and makes differences between the baseline and sequential model less sensitive to an unusually easy or hard holdout window.
corruptions.why_wrong: The data are time-ordered and the deployment setting is to predict on future sessions. Mixing sessions from all months into train, validation, and test lets the model benefit from contemporaneous marketplace patterns that would not be available at serving time, inflating performance estimates.

corruptions.original_text: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC). Justification: (1) AUROC is threshold-invariant and reflects the model's ability to rank-order sessions by conversion likelihood — directly aligned with the business goal of identifying high-intent visitors for targeted interventions. (2) AUROC is robust to class imbalance (unlike accuracy or F1 at a fixed threshold), so it fairly compares both models on the heavily imbalanced test set. (3) AUROC enables downstream users (marketing, product) to select an operating point (threshold) that balances precision and recall based on intervention budget, without the model being over-fit to a particular threshold.
corruptions.corrupted_text: Primary metric: Expected Calibration Error (ECE). ECE is the main selection and reporting metric because the downstream action is budget allocation: marketing teams will consume predicted probabilities directly to rank and size incentives, so probability fidelity matters more than pairwise ranking alone. A model that is slightly less separative but materially better calibrated is operationally preferable, since overconfident scores can systematically misallocate spend across traffic sources and session types.
corruptions.why_wrong: The stated goal is to identify high-intent visitors for targeted intervention, which is fundamentally a ranking problem under a limited budget. Using calibration as the primary model-selection criterion can favor a weaker ranker that is better calibrated overall, misaligning evaluation with the actual decision objective.

corrupted_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Sequential Clickstream Model for First-Time Buyer Conversion Prediction

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

This experiment uses anonymized session logs spanning 18 months from the production e-commerce platform. Each record represents a complete session: a visitor's journey from first page view to session exit or purchase. For each session, we retain the full ordered sequence of clickstream events (page type, search queries, product views, cart actions), per-event dwell times in seconds, device metadata (type, OS, browser), traffic source (acquisition channel), and a binary label indicating whether the visitor made a purchase within 30 calendar days of session start. The label is finalized after the 30-day attribution window closes, ensuring no future information leaks into training.

The data is known to be imbalanced: typically 2–5% of first-time sessions convert to purchase. We accept this and design all components (loss functions, metrics, splits) to handle imbalance explicitly rather than downsample or oversample, which would distort the temporal and channel distributions.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split and Stratification

We partition the 18 months of sessions into training (70%), validation (15%), and held-out test (15%) using stratified sampling over acquisition channel crossed with calendar month. This split design keeps each subset matched on both channel composition and seasonal demand regimes, which is important because conversion rates shift substantially across campaign cycles, product launches, and holiday periods. By preserving the month-by-channel mix in each subset, the comparison between the sequential model and the baseline is less sensitive to an idiosyncratically easy or difficult validation or test window.

This stratification serves two purposes: it prevents either model from benefiting simply from a more favorable acquisition mix in one split, and it supports reliable per-channel analysis at evaluation time because all major channels remain represented throughout training, validation, and test. In practice, this gives a cleaner estimate of whether sequence information adds value beyond static features under comparable traffic conditions.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

We construct two feature sets: **baseline (static) features** and **sequential features**.

**Baseline features** (used by both models): device type (mobile/desktop/tablet, one-hot encoded), operating system, browser, acquisition channel (one-hot or target-encoded), referrer domain, temporal features (hour of day, day of week, is_weekend), and session-level aggregates derived from the event sequence: total dwell time, mean dwell time per event, event count, and product category distribution (e.g., % of events on electronics, apparel, etc.).

**Sequential features** (for the sequential model only): the ordered sequence of event types (page view, search, product view, cart add, cart remove, checkout), product-level features if available (price bucket, rating), and per-event dwell times (clipped at the 99th percentile to prevent outliers from dominating embeddings).

All scaling and encoding transformers (StandardScaler for dwell times, OneHotEncoder for device type, target-encoding for traffic source) are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. This ensures we do not learn statistics from held-out data.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline Comparison

**Sequential model**: A 2-layer Transformer encoder with 8 attention heads ingests the ordered event sequence (each event is embedded as [event_type_embedding, dwell_time_normalized]) and produces a session-level representation via mean-pooling across the sequence. This representation is concatenated with baseline static features, passed through a 2-layer dense network (128 units each, ReLU, 0.4 dropout), and output via sigmoid for binary probability. The model is trained with class-weighted cross-entropy loss (positive class weight = total negatives / total positives) to account for imbalance.

**Baseline model**: Logistic regression trained on the baseline feature set only (no access to event sequence). Regularized with L2 penalty (C tuned on validation set). Both models receive equal hyperparameter tuning effort: we grid-search 50–100 configurations per model (learning rate, embedding dimension, dropout, L2 penalty, batch size) using the validation set ECE as the selection criterion. This ensures the comparison isolates the value of sequence information while favoring models whose probabilities can be used directly for downstream budget allocation.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: Expected Calibration Error (ECE).** ECE is chosen as the primary metric because the downstream use case consumes predicted probabilities directly: marketing and product teams will rank sessions and size interventions based on the confidence of conversion estimates, not just on relative ordering. In that setting, a model that assigns probabilities that closely match observed conversion frequencies is preferable to one that is more separative but systematically over- or under-confident. Using ECE as the main metric therefore aligns evaluation with the operational requirement that scores be trustworthy across channels, devices, and session types.

**Secondary metrics**: (a) AUROC, to quantify overall separability and confirm that any calibration gains do not come from a collapse in ranking performance; (b) Average Precision (area under the Precision-Recall curve), which is more sensitive to minority class performance and relevant for high-precision intervention scenarios; (c) per-channel calibration and AUROC stratification to detect channel-specific overfitting or miscalibration; (d) session-length stratified calibration and AUROC to control for the confound that the sequential model might simply learn 'longer sessions convert more.'

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set. For each model, we train on the training set with different hyperparameter configurations, evaluate each on validation ECE, and select the single best configuration. The test set is locked and untouched during this phase. At the end, both the baseline and sequential models are retrained on the combined training + validation data using the selected hyperparameters, then evaluated exactly once on the test set. This maximizes training signal while preserving a final held-out assessment.

### 7. Confound Control and Claim Scope

We control for confounds as follows: (1) the baseline model uses only static aggregates, so any improvement by the sequential model cannot be attributed to device, traffic source, or simple engagement metrics; (2) per-channel calibration and AUROC stratification on the test set confirms the sequential model generalizes across acquisition channels and is not exploiting channel-specific patterns; (3) session-length stratification confirms the model is learning intent signals, not just 'longer sessions convert;' (4) the stratified split keeps channel and seasonal composition balanced across development and evaluation subsets; (5) class-weighted loss in both models ensures fair comparison on imbalanced data.

This design establishes whether a sequential model trained on clickstream events and dwell times predicts 30-day purchase conversion better than a static baseline. It does **not** establish causation (the model correlates with intent, it does not cause it), nor does it quantify the offline impact of deploying this model on marketing interventions — that would require a randomized intervention experiment downstream.
